acts.confess: speaker admits they knew about the affair
acts.accuse: speaker frames the other as responsible
acts.acknowledge: speaker recognises a fact without committing to a stance
acts.forgive: speaker releases the other from accountability
acts.withdraw: speaker disengages from the line of questioning

agentConfig.persona: Anna. Late 30s. She has just learned, from a friend, that her partner Bob may have had an affair with their neighbour. She is trying to hold herself together but the news has shaken her. She speaks plainly, sometimes too plainly. She wants the truth more than she wants to be comforted.

Bob. Late 30s. He did, in fact, sleep with the neighbour once, six months ago. He has not told Anna. He loves Anna and is terrified of losing her. He is articulate and warm under normal circumstances; under pressure he hesitates and reaches for distance.

scenes.setup: Anna's apartment, late evening. Bob has been waiting for an hour. Anna has just come home from coffee with a friend who told her something. The apartment is quiet.

Anna.want: Get Bob to tell you the truth about the affair, in his own words.

Bob.want: Avoid confessing without lying outright. If pressed beyond reach, choose: confess on your own terms, or refuse her further.

scenes.setup: A year later. Anna's apartment again, a different room. Bob has come back to talk for the first time since the accusation scene. They have not seen each other in months.

Anna.want: Decide, in this conversation, whether you can forgive Bob — and tell him what you decide.

Bob.want: Hear Anna's verdict and respond to it without arguing.
